answer: This is because when you make an assignment to a variable in a scope, that variable becomes local to that scope and shadows any similarly named variable in the outer scope. Consequently when the earlier print(x) attempts to print the uninitialized local variable and an error results. You can access the outer scope variable by declaring it global, or in a nested scope using the nonlocal keyword.

If a mutable object like a list or dictionary is used as a default value for a function argument, that object is created only once when the function is defined, not each time the function is called. As a result, changes to the object persist across function calls, leading to unexpected behavior such as accumulating values between calls. The recommended workaround is to use None as the default value and inside the function, check if the parameter is None and create a new object if it is.

The += operator mutates lists in place by extending them, but for tuples and integers, it creates new objects. This difference exists because lists are mutable and implement the __iadd__ method, which modifies the list in place, while tuples and integers are immutable, so += creates a new object and attempts to assign it, which is not allowed for tuples and simply rebinds the variable for integers.

Four distinct methods are described: returning a tuple of results, using global variables, passing a mutable object (like a list), and passing a dictionary that gets mutated (or bundling values in a class instance). The recommended approach is to return a tuple containing the multiple results.

It is sometimes necessary to move imports to a function or class to avoid problems with circular imports. It may also be necessary to move imports out of the top level of code if some of the modules are platform-specific. Only move imports into a local scope, such as inside a function definition, if it’s necessary to solve a problem such as avoiding a circular import or are trying to reduce the initialization time of a module. This technique is especially helpful if many of the imports are unnecessary depending on how the program executes. Note that loading a module the first time may be expensive because of the one time initialization of the module, but loading a module multiple times is virtually free, costing only a couple of dictionary lookups.
